reading-error-messages.label: Reading Error Messages
reading-error-messages.summary: Learn how to read tracebacks and locate the line that needs attention.

q1.title: What does a traceback indicate?
q1.prompt: A traceback shows the sequence of function calls that led to an error.
q1.hint: Think about what information is provided when an error occurs.

help.concept: A traceback provides a detailed report of the error, including where it occurred.
help.hint_1: Look for the part of the traceback that describes the error.
help.hint_2: Focus on the last line of the traceback for the main error message.

options.b: The sequence of function calls that led to an error
options.c: The variables defined in the program

q2.title: Where should you look to find the specific line of code that caused an error?
q2.prompt: Identify the part of the traceback that points to the error in your code.
q2.hint: The last part of the traceback usually contains the line number.

help.concept: The last line of the traceback indicates where the error occurred in your code.
help.hint_1: Check the line number mentioned in the traceback.
help.hint_2: The bottom line typically describes the error type and location.

options.a: At the top of the traceback
options.b: In the middle of the traceback
options.c: At the bottom of the traceback
options.d: In the comments of the code

q3.title: Which of the following are common types of errors indicated in a traceback?
q3.hint: Think about the different types of errors you might encounter in Python.

help.concept: Tracebacks can indicate various types of errors that occur during execution.
help.hint_1: Consider the common errors you have learned about.
help.hint_2: All listed options are valid error types in Python.

q5.title: Fix the Error
q5.prompt: The following code produces an error. Identify and fix the issue.
q5.hint: Read the task "Fix the Error" and identify what the program should print.

help.concept: This coding exercise checks whether your code produces the requested output for: "Fix the Error".
help.hint_1: Use the Python statement or expression that matches the required output.
help.hint_2: Run the code and compare the output panel with the expected result.

1.title: Understanding Tracebacks
1.bodyMarkdown:
When a Python program encounters an error, it produces a traceback. This traceback shows the sequence of function calls that led to the error. Each line in the traceback indicates the file name, line number, and the code that was executed. 

**Example:**
```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  line 3, in code editor
    print(hello)
NameError: name 'hello' is not defined
```
In this example, the error occurs because the variable `hello` was not defined before it was used. 

**Your turn:** Try running a simple program with an intentional error to see how the traceback looks.

2.title: Locating the Error
2.bodyMarkdown:
To fix an error, first look at the last line of the traceback. This line usually contains the type of error and a description. The lines above it show where the error occurred in your code. 

**Example:**
If you see a `TypeError`, it means that an operation was applied to an object of an inappropriate type. Check the line number indicated in the traceback to find the problematic code. 

**Try this:** Modify your code to introduce a different error and observe how the traceback changes.
